name: diff->affected-test retrieval on a densified modifies graph (pilot cross-repo)
hypothesis: R16E found diff->test structure-bound at R@1 0.009 (47% of gold tests isolated after the leakage guard); R17b showed that ceiling is an ingest-depth artefact (real co-change -> 96.4% reachable). Does densifying the modifies graph convert that structural unlock into an actual retrieval result?
command: GITHUB_TOKEN=$GH_PAT python data/pilot/graph/densify_modifies.py && python data/pilot/graph/embed_dense_pr.py && PYTHONPATH=src python data/pilot/graph/run_diff2test_dense.py
leakage_checks: Gold (query-PR, test) modifies edges excluded from aggregation (leakage guard); test features come ONLY from NON-gold modifying PRs.
Dense edges live-ingested from the 8 held-out test repos; references scrubbed from PR text before embedding.
Same R16E scorer + guard with original vs original+dense edges — the original-edges cell reproduces the R16E floor exactly (R@1 0.0089), so the gain is purely the densification.
known_limitations: Pilot scale, single cross-repo split, one (alpha=0.5, hops=1) point. Test nodes carry no text embedding — the signal is purely structural (co-change geometry).

Reachability 89.3% (vs the 96.4% commit-level ceiling): PR-level densification (recent merged PRs touching test files) captures less than full commit history, so R@1 0.305 is a floor for this approach, not a ceiling. More PRs / commit-level edges would push it higher.
Dense graph is a one-time live snapshot (committed for offline replay). Exploratory.
